findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add two explicit test cases to the unit test for the hook's error path and fallback path: (a) assert `hatch_build.initialize` raises (or `python -m build` exits non-zero) when `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` is set to an empty string, and (b) assert the resulting wheel's `COMMIT` is a valid git short SHA when `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` is unset and a `.git` directory is present.
evidence: AC3 names two additional behaviors beyond the happy path but the only described test is 'asserts the wheel built via the default sdist→wheel path contains COMMIT equal to that short SHA'
No step in 'What' or 'Scope' describes a test case for empty-env-var failure or unset-env-var git fallback

The unit test in test_attestation_signing.py is described as covering 'the wheel-from-sdist path (the currently-untested COMMIT = None case)' — neither the empty-var nor unset-var paths are listed as test cases
ABSENCE: no `pytest.raises` / `subprocess` invocation with `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=` (empty) described anywhere
findings.finding: AC3 asserts two behaviors of the hook's fail-fast and fallback paths — (a) the build fails when REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT is set but empty/unresolvable, and (b) an unset env var degrades gracefully to git — but no test or proving command is described for either case; only the happy-path (env var set to a valid short SHA → wheel contains that SHA) is covered by the described unit test.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3, paragraph: 'The hook fails the build only when REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT is set but unresolvable (release context); an unset env var falls back to git (dev pip install . unaffected).'
scenarios: An implementer adds the env-var guard but inverts the condition (fails when unset instead of when set-but-empty); AC3 passes because the happy-path test passes but the regression goes undetected
The graceful-fallback claim is shipped untested; a later refactor breaks dev pip install . silently

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit test (in `tests/unit/test_attestation_signing.py` or a new hook test file) that sets `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=''` (or an unresolvable value) and asserts that running `hatch_build.py`'s `initialize` (or `python -m build`) raises / exits non-zero, covering the fail-fast contract introduced in AC3.
evidence: 'the hook raises (fails the build) only when REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT is set but empty/unresolvable' — What section

'Fail-fast scoped to the release context: the hook raises (fails the build) only when REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT is set but empty/unresolvable — so a developer's plain pip install . still degrades gracefully' — What section

AC3 only asserts 'hook fails the build only when REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT is set but unresolvable' as a REQUIREMENT, never as a TESTED criterion; no test description covers REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT='' or REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=<invalid>
ABSENCE: no test in tests/unit/test_attestation_signing.py or elsewhere is described that sets REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT to an empty string or invalid value and asserts that the build raises
findings.finding: The fail-fast behavior introduced in hatch_build.py (raise/fail the build when REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT is set but empty or unresolvable) is a new, user-facing failure mode with no test coverage specified anywhere in the plan. The ACs and test descriptions cover only success paths (non-null COMMIT assertions); no test for the failure-fast branch is described.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC3; Scope `hatch_build.py`; What section 'Fail-fast scoped to the release context'
scenarios: REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT is set to empty string in CI due to a misconfiguration — build should fail but no test catches this regression if the fail-fast branch is accidentally deleted or weakened
A future refactor of initialize() removes the raise accidentally; no test guards the contract
findings.suggested_fix: In the test additions to `tests/unit/test_attestation_signing.py`, add a parametrized case: `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=''` → assert `subprocess.run(['python', '-m', 'build'], env={**os.environ, 'REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT': ''})` exits with non-zero return code (or that calling `initialize()` directly raises). This is a pure in-process test with no live boundary, so no live/end-to-end complication applies.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the scope section that `force_include` for `_build_info.py` already exists in `hatch_build.py` line 51, and that the actual new work is the four-step precedence logic — not adding `force_include` (which is already there).
evidence: hatch_build.py line 51: `build_data.setdefault('force_include', {})[str(target)] = str(_TARGET)` — already present, unconditional
Plan: 'add `src/rebar/_build_info.py` to `build_data["force_include"]` so the generated (git-ignored) file is shipped in the sdist as well as the wheel'
The existing force_include call has no target-type guard, so it already runs for both wheel and sdist builds
findings.finding: The plan's scope section states 'add `src/rebar/_build_info.py` to `build_data["force_include"]`' as new work, but `hatch_build.py` line 51 already does exactly this (`build_data.setdefault("force_include", {})[str(target)] = str(_TARGET)`). The scope item as written implies this is net-new, which would mislead an implementer into thinking the current hook doesn't ship `_build_info.py` in the sdist — when it already unconditionally adds the file to `force_include` for all build targets including sdist.
scenarios: An implementer reads the scope item as confirming work to do and wastes time, or conversely skips reviewing whether the sdist actually ships the file today (the real gap is that _build_info.py has COMMIT=None when built outside git, not that force_include is missing).
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'add `src/rebar/_build_info.py` to `build_data["force_include"]`' with 'confirm/retain the existing `force_include` call (already present at line 51) and add the four-step precedence logic around it'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `r.files('rebar').rglob('*.json')` in the wheel-test probe with a `Traversable`-safe traversal (e.g. `r.files('rebar.schemas').iterdir()` filtering for `.json` suffix, or convert to `Path` first via `Path(str(r.files('rebar')))`) to avoid depending on `rglob` which is not part of the `Traversable` protocol.
evidence: Plan specifies: `python -c "import importlib.resources as r, json, rebar; [json.loads(f.read_text()) for f in r.files('rebar').rglob('*.json')]"` in the What section and AC2
`importlib.resources.Traversable` protocol defines: `iterdir()`, `open()`, `read_bytes()`, `read_text()`, `is_dir()`, `is_file()` — no `rglob`
All rglob usages in repo (tests/unit/test_canonical.py, tests/unit/test_core_optionality.py, etc.) target `pathlib.Path` objects, never `importlib.resources` traversables
Evidence class: Partially-verified — works on filesystem installs where files() returns a Path-like object, but not guaranteed by the Traversable contract
findings.finding: The plan specifies `r.files('rebar').rglob('*.json')` as a concrete probe command (in both the What section and AC2), but `importlib.resources.files()` returns a `Traversable` object whose protocol does NOT define `rglob()` — that method belongs to `pathlib.Path`. On a real-filesystem wheel install the underlying object may also be a `Path` and `rglob` would work incidentally, but this is backend-dependent and not part of the `Traversable` contract. The existing codebase never calls `rglob` on a `files()` result; all uses of `rglob` in the repo target explicit `pathlib.Path` objects. If the probe is run in a zip-imported or alternate-backend context the `AttributeError` would fail the wheel-test job with a misleading error unrelated to the actual artifact content.
findings.location: What section — wheel-test probe command; AC2 — sdist-test job probe
scenarios: Wheel installed from a zip-backed importer: `files('rebar')` returns a non-Path Traversable, `rglob` raises AttributeError, wheel-test job fails with a confusing error
Future Python version changes the Traversable implementation: the probe silently breaks
findings.suggested_fix: Use `pathlib.Path(str(r.files('rebar'))) .rglob('*.json')` (converting the Traversable to a concrete Path before calling rglob), or use `[f for f in r.files('rebar.schemas').iterdir() if f.name.endswith('.json')]` with a recursive helper — both work regardless of the importlib backend.

findings.finding: The wheel/sdist artifact test probe specifies `r.files('rebar').rglob('*.json')`, but `importlib.resources.abc.Traversable` — the type returned by `importlib.resources.files()` — does not define `rglob()`. The method does not exist on the Traversable interface; it only provides `iterdir()`, `is_dir()`, `is_file()`, `open()`, `read_bytes()`, `read_text()`, and `joinpath()`. This probe will raise `AttributeError` at runtime and fail the artifact test job for the wrong reason.
findings.location: What section — wheel-test probe command; AC line 2 (wheel-test + sdist-test job probes)
findings.suggested_fix: Use a recursive traversal helper instead: `def _rglob_json(t): return [f for e in t.iterdir() for f in ([e] if not e.is_dir() and str(e).endswith('.json') else _rglob_json(e) if e.is_dir() else [])]` — or, since the wheel is unpacked on a real filesystem, cast to `Path(str(importlib.resources.files('rebar')))` and use `Path.rglob('*.json')` directly.

findings.finding: The wheel-test probe command `r.files('rebar').rglob('*.json')` calls `.rglob()` directly on the `Traversable` object returned by `importlib.resources.files()`, but `Traversable` does not define `.rglob()` — that method lives on `pathlib.Path`. When the wheel is installed from a zip (the exact sdist→wheel→install path being tested), the Traversable is NOT a Path and the probe will raise `AttributeError`, making the test that is supposed to catch the COMMIT=None bug itself broken.
findings.location: What section / AC item 2: `python -c "import importlib.resources as r, json, rebar; [json.loads(f.read_text()) for f in r.files('rebar').rglob('*.json')]"` probe command
findings.suggested_fix: Use `from importlib.resources import files; import pathlib; schemas = pathlib.Path(str(files('rebar.schemas'))); [json.loads(f.read_text()) for f in schemas.glob('*.schema.json')]` — this matches the established pattern in `src/rebar/schemas/__init__.py` and works on both editable and zip installs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (with a citation to Hatchling docs or a spike test) that `build_data['force_include']` is honored by the Hatchling sdist builder, and add this as a precondition note in the plan — or, if it is NOT honored, prescribe the alternative mechanism (e.g., a `get_include_paths` override or an explicit `[tool.hatch.build.targets.sdist] include` entry for `src/rebar/_build_info.py`).
evidence: Plan states: 'the hook's `force_include` works for both targets' and 'Do NOT add `artifacts=` to the sdist pyproject target — Hatchling's sdist builder uses `include`/`exclude` only and silently ignores `artifacts`'.

Existing `hatch_build.py` already sets `build_data.setdefault('force_include', {})[str(target)] = str(_TARGET)` unconditionally — but the current sdist build may or may not honor it (the defect described in the plan implies the sdist currently does NOT ship `_build_info.py`, which would mean `force_include` is NOT currently working for sdist, contradicting the plan's assertion that it 'works for both targets').

The plan says 'Without this the sdist never contains the baked SHA' — implying the current code (which already sets force_include) is insufficient for sdist, yet the proposed fix is only to 'add' it, which is already present.
Hatchling docs distinguish `build_data['force_include']` behavior between wheel and sdist targets — sdist builders process `force_include` differently from wheel builders in some versions.
findings.finding: The plan's central mechanism for getting the baked SHA into the sdist depends on `build_data['force_include']` working in Hatchling's sdist builder, but the plan asserts this without citing documentation or a test proving it — the current hook code sets `force_include` unconditionally for both build targets, yet the sdist `[tool.hatch.build.targets.sdist]` `include` list does not contain `_build_info.py`, suggesting the sdist builder may need explicit verification that `force_include` in `build_data` is honored.
findings.location: Scope section — '`hatch_build.py` (`initialize`) — add `src/rebar/_build_info.py` to `build_data["force_include"]` so the generated (git-ignored) file is shipped in the sdist as well as the wheel.'
scenarios: If `force_include` is silently ignored for the sdist builder (as `artifacts=` is), the sdist will never contain `_build_info.py`, the preserve-existing path has nothing to preserve, and the `COMMIT = None` bug persists for all `pip install <sdist>` users.
The sdist-test job (AC2) will fail its non-null COMMIT assert even after the fix, making the entire build-once pipeline broken at launch.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the `initialize` precedence to prefer `git rev-parse --short HEAD` over preserve-existing when `.git` is present (i.e., order: env-var → git-if-.git-present → preserve-existing-if-no-.git → None), so repeated dev installs always reflect the current HEAD while the sdist→wheel path (no `.git`, no env var) still falls through to preserve the baked SHA.
evidence: '(2) else preserve an existing non-null COMMIT already in _build_info.py if one is present; (3) else git rev-parse --short HEAD'
Plan claim: 'a developer's plain pip install . (env var unset) still degrades gracefully to the git value and never breaks' — contradicted by step (2) firing whenever the file exists
ABSENCE: no cleanup/invalidation path for a stale `_build_info.py` in a dev checkout across commits — the file is git-ignored so it persists silently across branch switches and HEAD changes
findings.finding: The four-step precedence (`REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` → preserve-existing → git → None) causes stale-SHA freezing for repeated local `pip install .` across commits: once `_build_info.py` exists with any non-null SHA, step (2) preserves it on every subsequent install instead of falling through to step (3)'s `git rev-parse --short HEAD`. The plan's own stated guarantee — 'a developer's plain `pip install .` degrades gracefully to the git value' — is only true on the very first install when the file is absent.
findings.location: What section — 'Fix the hook — a four-step precedence (env var + preserve-existing + git)'; Scope — `hatch_build.py`; AC line: 'The hook fails the build only when REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT is set but unresolvable (release context); an unset env var falls back to git (dev pip install . unaffected)'
scenarios: Developer is on commit A, runs `pip install .`, gets `_build_info.py` with SHA=`abc1234`. Switches to commit B, runs `pip install .` again — step (2) preserves `abc1234` (non-null, file exists) and step (3) is never reached, so the installed package reports the wrong commit.
A CI matrix job that re-uses a workspace across steps could bake an earlier commit's SHA into a later step's install.
findings.suggested_fix: Reorder: (1) `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` env var; (2) `git rev-parse --short HEAD` if `.git` is reachable (covers dev installs at any commit); (3) preserve an existing non-null `COMMIT` in `_build_info.py` if `.git` is NOT present (covers sdist→wheel rebuild path); (4) `None`. This keeps the sdist install-time guarantee intact while fixing the dev stale-SHA problem and keeping the plan's stated guarantee accurate.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Guard step (2) 'preserve-existing' so it only fires when no live `.git` tree is present at the build root (i.e., check `Path(root).joinpath('.git').exists()` is False before preserving); in a live git checkout with `.git` present, always fall through to step (3)'s `git rev-parse --short HEAD` so repeated `pip install .` across commits always bakes the current HEAD SHA.
evidence: '(2) else preserve an existing non-null COMMIT already in _build_info.py if one is present; (3) else git rev-parse --short HEAD'

'a developer's plain pip install . (env var unset) still degrades gracefully to the git value and never breaks' — this claim is only true on the first install when _build_info.py is absent; after that, step (2) always fires first
_build_info.py is git-ignored (confirmed in existing hatch_build.py docstring and pyproject.toml) so it persists silently across commits, branch switches, and rebases
The fix the plan introduces does not gate step (2) on the absence of a live .git tree
findings.finding: The four-step hook precedence has a mechanism flaw: step (2) 'preserve an existing non-null COMMIT' fires in live git checkouts whenever `_build_info.py` was previously written, causing the baked SHA to freeze at the first-install commit and never update as the developer advances HEAD — directly contradicting the plan's stated guarantee that 'a developer's plain `pip install .` (env var unset) still degrades gracefully to the git value'.
findings.location: What section — 'Fix the hook — a four-step precedence (env var + preserve-existing + git)'; Scope — `hatch_build.py`; AC3
scenarios: Developer on commit A runs `pip install .` → _build_info.py written with SHA=abc1234. Developer advances to commit B, runs `pip install .` again → step (2) preserves abc1234 (non-null, file exists), step (3) never reached → installed package permanently reports wrong commit until _build_info.py is manually deleted.
A CI job that reuses a workspace across matrix steps bakes an earlier step's SHA into a later step's install, producing incorrect provenance in a context where the env var is not set.
findings.suggested_fix: In `hatch_build.py`'s `initialize`, after checking `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT`, only read and preserve the existing `_build_info.py` value if `(Path(self.root) / '.git').exists()` is False — i.e., wrap step (2) in `if not (root / '.git').exists(): ...`. In a git checkout the preserve-existing path is skipped and step (3) always runs, keeping dev installs accurate; in an extracted sdist (no .git) step (2) fires and keeps the baked SHA through the rebuild.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State an explicit default decision for the test.yml probe: either 'add it in this story' (with a concrete AC or a sub-bullet describing what the probe must assert) or 'defer to a named follow-up' (with a one-line rationale such as 'release job already covers this path; regular CI probe is a separate story').
evidence: The word 'optionally' makes this a live design decision deferred to the executor
No AC covers this scope bullet
No rationale is provided for why it would be skipped (e.g. 'skip if build time budget exceeds N min'; 'defer to a follow-up story')
The motivating sentence ('so the defect is caught pre-release') suggests it is valuable, making the omission more confusing
findings.finding: The Scope section marks the test.yml addition as 'optionally add a wheel/sdist artifact probe to regular CI' with no AC, no default decision, and no rationale for skipping — leaving the executor to make a real design choice (do it or not) without guidance.
findings.location: Scope section — '.github/workflows/test.yml — optionally add a wheel/sdist artifact probe to regular CI so the defect is caught pre-release, not only in the release run.'
scenarios: One implementer adds the probe and extends CI run time; another skips it; neither is wrong per the plan, creating inconsistent outcomes across re-executions
A reviewer asks why test.yml was not updated; the implementer who skipped it has no plan text to cite
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'optionally add a wheel/sdist artifact probe to regular CI' with either: (a) 'Add a wheel-install smoke probe to test.yml nightly/PR matrix: install the wheel built in the same run and assert `python -c "from rebar import _build_info; assert _build_info.COMMIT"`' (and add a matching AC), or (b) 'Out of scope for this story — deferred to <follow-up>; the release job's two artifact-test jobs provide the same coverage for every release.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC criterion for the optional test.yml wheel/sdist probe (even a conditional one such as 'if test.yml is modified, the probe must run the same concrete assertions as the wheel-test job'), or explicitly move it out of scope for this story.
evidence: Scope: 'optionally add a wheel/sdist artifact probe to regular CI so the defect is caught pre-release, not only in the release run'
No AC criterion references test.yml or the optional probe.
findings.finding: The plan marks 'optionally add a wheel/sdist artifact probe to regular CI (test.yml)' as in-scope but provides no acceptance criterion covering it. An implementer who adds it has no criterion to pass; one who skips it has no criterion requiring explanation. This is an orphan in-scope deliverable with no AC coverage.
findings.location: Scope section (test.yml bullet) and Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding criterion)
scenarios: Implementer adds test.yml probe but the review has no AC to verify it against.
Implementer skips it and the review has no AC that explicitly accepts the omission.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: 'If test.yml is modified, the new probe job installs the built wheel/sdist, runs the same five concrete assertions as the wheel-test job, and is skipped (not failed) when no dist/ artifacts are present (e.g., PRs without a release build).' Alternatively, strike the bullet from Scope and note it as future work.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `github_release` and `mcp_registry` jobs in release.yml as consumers that must be updated when the artifact bundle name/structure changes, and specify the required edits to their artifact download steps in the Scope section.
evidence: release.yml:85: `- uses: actions/download-artifact@v8` with `name: dist` in github_release job
release.yml:85-88: github_release downloads `name: dist`
Scope section: 'coordinate with Findings 1 and 4, which also edit this file' — no mention of github_release or mcp_registry as consumers
AC1: 'the release builds artifacts once and uploads a single named bundle' — implies the artifact name changes from `dist`
mcp_registry job (release.yml:109) also `needs: publish` and uses the same dist artifact path indirectly via github_release pattern
findings.finding: The plan restructures the build-once job to upload a new named artifact bundle (replacing the existing `dist` artifact name) and rewires `publish`'s `needs:`. However, the `github_release` job (release.yml:75) and `mcp_registry` job (release.yml:109) both currently `needs: publish` and download the artifact named `dist` — they are not named anywhere in the plan's Scope or What sections as consumers that require updating. When the artifact bundle is renamed or its structure changes, these two jobs will break.
scenarios: The build-once job uploads `dist-bundle` (or any name other than `dist`); the `github_release` job still requests `name: dist` and fails with artifact-not-found.
The publish job gains a `needs: [wheel-test, sdist-test]` but `github_release` still only `needs: publish` — this is fine for ordering but the artifact name download must still be updated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section under `.github/workflows/release.yml`: 'The `github_release` and `mcp_registry` jobs currently download the `dist` artifact by name — update both to download from the renamed build-once bundle (or re-upload a `dist`-compatible artifact in the publish job so downstream jobs are unaffected).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief 'Emergency back-out' note to docs/releasing.md (or the plan's Scope) describing how maintainers can bypass or revert the new workflow gates if a false failure blocks a release — e.g., 'revert the release.yml PR and re-run the release from the prior workflow' — satisfying T4's requirement for an explicit rollback mechanism.
evidence: AC criterion 1: 'publish downloads that same bundle and runs sha256sum -c SHA256SUMS (failing on any mismatch) before publishing — no rebuild occurs in publish'
AC criterion 2: describes wheel-test and sdist-test as required gates
No section describes an emergency bypass or back-out procedure if the new gates produce a false failure during a live release.
findings.finding: The plan restructures release.yml to require three new jobs (build-once, wheel-test, sdist-test) as gates before publish, but provides no rollback/back-out path for the release workflow itself if the new gates exhibit false failures (e.g., resource limits, network flakiness in the sdist-test clean-dir install) that block a time-sensitive release. T4 requires an explicit rollback mechanism, not just acknowledgement of the change.
findings.location: What section ('Publish the same bytes' bullet); Scope section (release.yml bullet); AC criterion 1 and 2
scenarios: A release is in flight; the sdist-test job fails due to a transient network issue in the clean-dir pip install; the team needs to ship a security fix and has no documented way to bypass the new gate without reverting the workflow PR.
findings.suggested_fix: In docs/releasing.md under the 'Build-once' section, add a short 'Incident recovery' subsection: 'If a wheel-test or sdist-test gate fails spuriously, revert the release.yml change (git revert <sha>), re-trigger the release tag, and file a bug against the flaky step. Do not manually edit SHA256SUMS to bypass the hash gate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the four-step hook precedence, the REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT short-SHA contract, and the force_include-not-artifacts= choice as a design-decision note in CLAUDE.md (or a new ADR) so future contributors have architectural rationale when modifying hatch_build.py.
evidence: 'docs/releasing.md — section ownership: this story owns the Build-once section, AND rewrites the existing Post-release smoke test section'

'Do NOT add artifacts= to the sdist pyproject target — Hatchling's sdist builder uses include/exclude only and silently ignores artifacts, a wheel-only key; the hook's force_include works for both targets.'
No mention of ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc entry for the hook precedence or env-var contract.
findings.finding: The four-step hook precedence, the REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT env-var contract, and the force_include-for-sdist pattern (and the explicit non-use of the silently-ignored `artifacts=` key) are novel architectural decisions captured only in the operational docs/releasing.md — not in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or any persistent design-decision record. A future contributor modifying hatch_build.py cold will have no architectural rationale explaining why the precedence is four steps, why the env var format is a 7-char short SHA, or why force_include is used instead of artifacts=.
findings.location: Scope section (hatch_build.py bullets) and docs/releasing.md ownership declaration
scenarios: A future contributor sees the four-step if/elif chain in hatch_build.py and simplifies it, breaking sdist installs for end-users who pip install from PyPI's sdist.
A future contributor 'fixes' force_include to use artifacts= because it looks more explicit, silently breaking sdist provenance.
A future contributor removes the REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT env-var injection from release.yml thinking it is redundant with the git fallback, breaking the release-context fail-fast guarantee.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Build provenance hook` section to CLAUDE.md (or `docs/adr/NNN-build-once-provenance.md`) explaining: (1) why four steps exist and what each protects; (2) why REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT uses ${GITHUB_SHA::7} (short-SHA convention matching signing.py); (3) why force_include is used instead of artifacts= in the sdist builder; (4) the failure mode if any step is removed. This is a 20-line addition but prevents repeated re-introduction of the None-commit defect.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the exact filename that Finding 4 will introduce for `test_release_workflow_pins.py` (or add a note that this story must wait for F4's merge and reference F4's AC that creates the file), so AC4's 'extend check (f)' has an unambiguous, verifiable target.
evidence: Plan: 'the `test_release_workflow_pins.py` test introduced by Finding 4 (available since this story `depends_on` F4)'
Search for 'test_release_workflow_pins' returns only cache files, not any file in tests/unit/
tests/unit/ directory listing contains no test_release_workflow_pins.py
findings.finding: The plan states 'the `test_release_workflow_pins.py` test introduced by Finding 4 (available since this story `depends_on` F4)' as a pre-existing file this story will extend. No such file exists in `tests/unit/` in the current codebase. This is an assertion about a future artifact from a dependency ticket, but if F4 has not yet landed — or lands without that exact filename — AC4's extension step has no target file and the AC as written cannot be satisfied.
findings.location: What section — 'Complete the F4 evidence handshake' bullet; AC4
scenarios: F4 lands with a differently named test file — AC4 extension target doesn't exist, leaving the evidence-artifacts wiring check unimplemented.
This story is reviewed or implemented before F4 is merged — implementer has no file to extend and may create a duplicate or skip AC4.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a cross-reference to the specific F4 AC or file-creation step that produces `tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py`, or rename the pre-condition to say 'the file created by F4 AC-X' to make the dependency concrete and verifiable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief prior-art note in the plan (or a comment in `hatch_build.py`) acknowledging `hatch-vcs` and explaining why it was not adopted (e.g., it writes `__version__`, not a custom `_build_info.COMMIT`; or the short-SHA format and fail-fast behavior require custom logic that hatch-vcs cannot provide).
evidence: Plan introduces a custom REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT env var + preserve-existing + git fallback + fail-fast chain without mentioning hatch-vcs.
`hatch-vcs` is the standard Hatchling plugin for VCS-derived build metadata; it already handles the sdist→wheel re-run case via its own write-then-preserve mechanism.
pyproject.toml [build-system] uses hatchling with a custom hook — no hatch-vcs entry, but no rejection rationale either.
ABSENCE: nowhere in the plan or pyproject.toml is hatch-vcs considered or dismissed.
findings.finding: `hatch-vcs` (the official Hatchling VCS plugin) solves exactly the problem this plan's bespoke four-step hook addresses — baking the commit SHA at build time and preserving it through the sdist→wheel rebuild — yet the plan introduces a custom hook without acknowledging `hatch-vcs` or explaining why it was ruled out.
findings.location: What section — 'Fix the hook — a four-step precedence (env var + preserve-existing + git)'; Scope — `hatch_build.py`
scenarios: A reviewer or future maintainer asks 'why not hatch-vcs?' and the plan has no answer, making the bespoke solution look like accidental reinvention.
hatch-vcs's sdist-preservation behavior may be better tested and maintained than the bespoke hook, so adopting it could eliminate the need to write and maintain the preserve-existing logic.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: '`hatch-vcs` was considered and ruled out because: (1) it writes `__version__` not a custom `_build_info.COMMIT`; (2) the short-SHA canonical format and release-context fail-fast are not configurable in hatch-vcs; (3) the project already has a working custom hook — this story extends it rather than replacing it with a new build dependency.'
